answer:
In Ghalib's ghazal "Dil laga kar lag gaya unko bhi tanha baithna," the sun is likened to a lamp swaying precariously in the wind, underscoring the universal transience and fragility of all existence.[^1]  

Similarly, in Zauq's ghazal "Aankh us pur-jafa se ladti hai," the candle contends against the passing wind in the beloved's absence, evoking the lover's turbulent instability.[^2]  

**Divergence:** Both poets deploy the flickering lamp/candle battered by wind as a metaphor for endurance amid chaos, but Ghalib elevates it to a cosmic meditation on inevitable decay ("All elements of being are destined to degrade"), where even the sun succumbs. Zauq, by contrast, grounds it in romantic longing, personalizing the struggle as the flame's (and lover's) defiant yet vulnerable resistance to separation ("lacking you"). Ghalib's scope is philosophical and impersonal; Zauq's intimate and emotional.[^3]

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/34-dil-laga-kar-lag-gaya-unko-bhi-tanha-baithna/translation/#p-6b9c2c).

[^2]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [paragraph](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/01-aankh-us-pur-jafa-se-ladti-hai/translation/#p-5b3765).

[^3]: For context on the shared radif/radeef structure and thematic overlap in classical Urdu ghazals, see the full chapters: Ghalib [ch. 34](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/34-dil-laga-kar-lag-gaya-unko-bhi-tanha-baithna/translation/); Zauq [ch. 1](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/01-aankh-us-pur-jafa-se-ladti-hai/translation/).
